steps.title: Identify the structure requiring punctuation
steps.content: The sentence reads “including the ______ Javanese and Bikol, which are spoken by…” The blank determines what appears between “the” and the names of two specific languages. The question is whether punctuation should separate the noun from its direct identifiers.

steps.title: Match the correct punctuation choice
steps.content:
When a noun is directly followed by the specific examples or names that identify it, no punctuation is required.

Compare: “the colors red and blue” or “the months January and February”—no commas needed because the names directly specify what the noun refers to.

Here, “the languages Javanese and Bikol” works the same way.

A comma would form an appositive structure that treats the names as secondary, a colon would introduce a formal list, and an em-dash would create awkward separation.

None of these fit when examples directly identify the noun. That matches choice A — A is correct.
